clusters.cluster_summary: These results isolate how derivations behave when the context is a singleton atom, a singleton formula, or an all-atomic context. core_dossier.headline_claim: Exact and irreducible classification of derivability from singleton and all-atomic contexts
core_dossier.core_mathematical_content: The cluster identifies the precise form of sequents derivable when the antecedent is a singleton formula, a singleton atom, or an arbitrary nonempty atomic context. In these regimes, derivability is completely characterized by a small family of cases: either the sequent is the atomic identity case, or the conclusion is obtained by a left-residual or right-residual extension whose premises are again derivable. For singleton antecedents this yields exact if-and-only-if case splits, and for all-atomic contexts it yields an exact context-local description of every derivable conclusion. The same cluster also shows that this exact classifier cannot be replaced by simpler syntactic summaries such as antecedent-conclusion equality, degree comparison, atom-count bounds, atom-occurrence inheritance, or a naive fold-normal-form recipe.

literature_context.closest_baseline: Typelogical Grammar (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy), as the clearest anchor for standard Lambek-style sequent calculi and routine residual decomposition
literature_context.routine_reading_risk: If stated only as a singleton or atomic-context case split, an outsider can read the result as standard slash-rule unpacking or repeated residuation in a mature Lambek proof-theoretic setting.
literature_context.possible_opening: The plausible theorem-level opening is to present the result as a minimal complete classifier together with an impossibility frontier: among natural context-local descriptors, this exact residual case analysis is complete, while all simpler candidates verified in the cluster are provably incomplete.

supporting_claims: A singleton antecedent derives an atomic conclusion exactly in the identity case: ([A] ⇒ #s) iff A = #s.
A singleton atomic antecedent has an exact derivability classifier: ([#s] ⇒ A) iff A is #s or a left/right residual extension backed by a derivable two-formula premise.
A general singleton antecedent has the analogous exact three-way classifier: identity or one left/right residual extension.
For nonempty all-atomic contexts, derivability admits an exact case split into a singleton-atom base case or a left/right residual extension of the context.

core_dossier.headline_claim: Handed support closure exactly captures derivability, whereas coarse support closure does not.
core_dossier.core_mathematical_content: The cluster isolates a strict boundary between two support-style semantics for sequents. Full derivability is equivalent to CandidateTree existence and also equivalent to HandedSupportClosure. By contrast, ordinary SupportClosure coincides only with the weaker admissibility predicate support_ok and is not extensionally equal to derivability. Residualization shows that the exact handed side reduces to an atomic state test: a sequent is derivable exactly when its residual atomic state exists and supports an AtomicCandidateTree, with reconstruction back to a full CandidateTree. On the coarse side, residualization exposes only atom-occurrence support inside the residual context, which explains why it fails to recover derivability.

core_dossier.core_mathematical_content: For any residual root obtained from a sequent, every reachable residual state preserves the distinguished atom, stays within the subformulas of the source sequent, and moves by strict descent of list degree; therefore the exact reachable region is well-founded and finite. But these local invariants do not by themselves recover exact reachability: the larger atom-fixed subformula slice can be infinite, and for arbitrary subsets of that slice finiteness holds exactly when there is a uniform degree bound. So the exact reachable region is a proper finite subregion cut out inside a much larger ambient state space with the same obvious local invariants.

core_dossier.core_mathematical_content: For every residual root state p, the exact reachable region R(p) is finite and degree-descending, and the accepting states inside R(p) are determined intrinsically rather than procedurally. The same subset is characterized in three forced ways: as the states that reach the distinguished singleton base state ([#(p.2)], p.2), as the least predecessor-closed subset of R(p) containing that base, and as the unique Boolean labeling on R(p) satisfying the local base-or-successor recursion. This classifier is independent of the chosen reverse-topological or degree-sorted sweep used to recover it. Terminal states fit this invariant sharply: a terminal reachable state is accepting exactly when it is the singleton base, and non-acceptance is witnessed at terminal rejecting frontiers.

headline: Handedness Is the Exact Support Invariant for Derivability, While Coarse Support Is Intrinsically Too Weak
package_strategy: Split the package into a short boundary sequence. Start by identifying the exact meaning of ordinary support closure, so the coarse notion is pinned down before any comparison with derivability. Next isolate the positive exactness theorem for handed support. Then state the paper-facing boundary theorem as the contrast between those two nearby semantics. After the headline is in place, use residualization and candidate-tree reconstruction only as explanatory support: residual normal form shows where the two closures diverge, and residual witness reconstruction explains why the handed side remains exact. Finish with a robustness corollary showing that the exact handed invariant survives standard one-sided reductions, while the coarse/handed gap does not disappear on same-handed fragments.

theorem_units.statement: For every context Γ and formula A, ordinary SupportClosure Γ A holds if and only if support_ok Γ A holds.
theorem_units.docstring_summary: Ordinary support closure captures exactly the coarse admissibility predicate support_ok.
theorem_units.purpose: This is the entry point because it gives the coarse support semantics a precise mathematical meaning before comparing it to derivability. It prevents the later negative result from sounding merely existential or anecdotal.
needs_new_ingredients: A clean clause-by-clause comparison between the SupportClosure rules and the recursive definition of support_ok
Local inversion lemmas matching each support constructor with the corresponding support_ok witness
proof_idea: First unfold SupportClosure and support_ok into their atomic, left-division, and right-division cases.
Then prove SupportClosure implies support_ok by induction on the support-closure derivation, checking that each local update preserves the support_ok condition.
Next prove support_ok implies SupportClosure by rebuilding a support-closure witness from the same case analysis on the target formula.
Finally package the two directions as an exact characterization of the coarse closure.

theorem_units.statement: For every context Γ and formula A, HandedSupportClosure Γ A holds if and only if the sequent Γ ⇒ A is derivable.
theorem_units.docstring_summary: Handed support closure is extensionally exact for derivability on all sequents.
theorem_units.purpose: This is the positive exactness half of the package. It isolates the extra invariant that succeeds where coarse support does not.
needs_new_ingredients: A clean bridge between candidate-tree branching data and the handed support conditions
Handed inversion lemmas that reconstruct a derivation or candidate-tree node from each handed-support step
proof_idea: First reduce derivability of Γ ⇒ A to existence of a CandidateTree Γ A.
Then show that each candidate-tree branch determines the correct handed support obligations, yielding HandedSupportClosure from any candidate tree.
For the converse, induct on a handed-support witness and reconstruct the corresponding candidate-tree node or derivation fragment at each step.
Finally translate the reconstructed candidate tree back into a derivation of Γ ⇒ A.

theorem_units.statement: Handedness is exactly the missing invariant needed to recover derivability from support semantics: HandedSupportClosure Γ A is equivalent to Γ ⇒ A for all sequents, but ordinary SupportClosure is not equivalent to derivability in general.
theorem_units.docstring_summary: The boundary theorem: handed support is exact for derivability, while coarse support is provably inexact.
theorem_units.purpose: This is the paper-facing headline. It presents the positive and negative sides together as a structural boundary inside a family of nearby support semantics, rather than as separate proof-search reformulations.
needs_new_ingredients: A clean packaged comparison corollary that juxtaposes the positive exactness theorem with the coarse non-exactness theorem
A stable exposition of the counterexample or abstract failure witness behind coarse non-exactness
proof_idea: First apply the handed exactness result to identify HandedSupportClosure with derivability on the nose.
Then apply the coarse characterization to identify ordinary SupportClosure with the weaker support_ok semantics.
Next invoke the non-exactness theorem for coarse support to rule out any universal equivalence between SupportClosure and derivability.
Finally conclude that handedness is precisely the extra invariant that upgrades support semantics from admissibility to exact derivability.

theorem_units.statement: For every sequent Γ ⇒ A, residualization to an atomic state separates the two support semantics exactly: HandedSupportClosure Γ A holds if and only if there is a residual atomic state (Δ, s) for Γ and A with an AtomicCandidateTree Δ s, whereas SupportClosure Γ A holds if and only if there is such a residual state in which the target atom s merely occurs somewhere in Δ.
theorem_units.docstring_summary: Residual normal form exposes the exact semantic reason handed support succeeds and coarse support fails.
theorem_units.purpose: This unit explains the boundary theorem concretely. It shows that the positive and negative sides already diverge at the residual atomic interface, so the gap is structural rather than presentational.
needs_new_ingredients: A clean residual-state statement that isolates the handed witness from the coarse atom-occurrence witness
Local lemmas relating residualAtomicState to the atomic support and candidate-tree interfaces
proof_idea: First reduce the original sequent to its residual atomic state whenever residualAtomicState Γ A is defined.
Then show that on the handed side, the residual problem is equivalent to derivability of the residual atomic sequent and hence to existence of an AtomicCandidateTree.
Next unwind ordinary SupportClosure through the same residualization and check that only occurrence of the target atom in the residual context remains.
Finally record the two residual characterizations side by side, making the exactness gap visible at the atomic level.

theorem_units.statement: Whenever residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s), atomic witnesses for the residual state reconstruct bijectively to full CandidateTree proofs of Γ ⇒ A.
theorem_units.docstring_summary: Residual atomic success is not merely a test; it reconstructs the full proof object for the original sequent.
theorem_units.purpose: This gives the explanatory support promised in the dossier without making candidate trees the headline. It shows why the handed residual criterion is genuinely complete rather than only sound.
needs_new_ingredients: Named forward and backward reconstruction maps between residual witnesses and full candidate trees
A clean statement that the reconstruction is inverse to residualization on the relevant witness space
proof_idea: First start from a residual witness (Δ, s) together with an AtomicCandidateTree Δ s.
Then apply the reconstruction map to build a CandidateTree Γ A for the original sequent.
Next verify the inverse direction by sending any reconstructed candidate tree back to its residual witness and checking that no information is lost.
Finally combine the bijection with CandidateTree exactness to interpret residual atomic witnesses as full proofs of Γ ⇒ A.

theorem_units.statement: On left-only and right-only fragments, handed support closure is preserved by the standard one-sided translations; nevertheless ordinary SupportClosure still does not collapse to the exact handed notion even on same-handed fragments.
theorem_units.docstring_summary: The exact handed invariant survives directional reductions, while the coarse/handed gap remains structural.
theorem_units.purpose: This closes the package by showing that the boundary theorem is robust under familiar one-sided reductions and is not an artifact of the two-sided presentation.
needs_new_ingredients: Fragment lemmas showing that the relevant left-only and right-only hypotheses are preserved by translation
A short comparison corollary that links the translated handed exactness result back to the global boundary theorem
proof_idea: First restrict to left-only or right-only sequents and apply the one-sided conservativity theorem for the translated presentation.
Then replace derivability by handed support using the handed exactness theorem, obtaining preservation of the exact handed invariant across translation.
Next invoke the same-handed failure theorem to show that coarse support still does not become exact merely by restricting to one directional side.
Finally conclude that the coarse/handed separation is structural and survives standard directional reductions.
